The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Begin the story as an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. Arthur meets with this unknown party, who makes an offer. This early scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The first chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. The remainder of the story is open to your choices & direction.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Task: write a short story over several chapters. Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra&#x27;s secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Begin the story as an armed retinue descends on Arthur&#x27;s home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. Arthur meets with this unknown party, who makes an offer. This early scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description &amp; scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes &amp; dangers. Include Asimov&#x27;s trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The first chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he&#x27;s gotten involved in. The remainder of the story is open to your choices &amp; direction. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# **Writing Plan for *The Azra Gambit*** **A Classic Sci-Fi Short Story in the Style of Isaac Asimov** --- ## **# Brainstorming** ### **1. Characters** **Arthur Neegan (Protagonist)** - **Background:** First-generation Martian settler, mining engineer with a modest claim. Pragmatic, self-reliant, but not a revolutionary. Has a dry, understated sense of humor. - **Motivations:** Wants to secure his future and that of his sister (if she exists) or a small community. Initially just wants to be left alone, but is drawn into larger conflicts. - **Flaws:** Stubborn, distrustful of authority, slow to recognize the scale of what he’s stumbled into. - **Arc:** From a man who just wants to mine in peace to someone who must navigate corporate espionage, political manipulation, and the ethical weight of Azra’s power. **The Antares Envoy (Antagonist? Ally? Wild Card?)** - **Name:** *Commander Elias Voss* (or a more bureaucratic title like *Special Attaché Voss*) - **Appearance:** Impeccably dressed in a retrofuturistic diplomatic uniform, gloves, a high-collared coat with the Antares sigil—a stylized red starburst. Speaks in measured, precise tones, but his eyes are sharp, calculating. - **Personality:** Charming, but in the way a scalpel is charming—precise, controlled, and dangerous if mishandled. Represents a faction that wants Azra for "stability" (i.e., their version of order). - **Secrets:** Is he truly Antares? Or is he a rogue operative? Does he have his own agenda beyond his superiors’ orders? **Corporate Interests** - **The Mars Consolidated Mining Guild (MCMG):** A cartel of Earth-based megacorps that control most of Mars’ leases. Ruthless, but bound by interplanetary treaties. - **The Free Martian Collective (FMC):** A loose alliance of independent miners and settlers who want to break the Guild’s monopoly. More idealistic, but also more violent. - **The Terran Accord:** The Earth government’s regulatory body, which claims oversight but is often outmaneuvered by corporations. **Other Key Figures** - **Dr. Lien Zhao:** A physicist who studied Azra’s properties. She’s either a prisoner, a defector, or a double agent. Knows the *real* dangers of Azra. - **Captain Rivas:** A mercenary or smuggler who offers Arthur an alternative to the Antares deal. Rough around the edges, but has a code. - **The "Ghost" of Azra:** A rumor—some say the first miners who found Azra went mad, others say they *ascended*. Is there a sentient force tied to the metal? Or is it just radiation poisoning? --- ### **2. Plot & Story Beats** **Themes to Explore:** - **Power & Corruption:** Azra is a MacGuffin that corrupts everyone who touches it—corporations, governments, even idealists. - **Colonialism & Exploitation:** Mars is a resource colony, and its people are either indentured laborers or small-time operators like Arthur. - **Science vs. Ethics:** Azra’s properties could revolutionize energy, but at what cost? (Asimov’s *Foundation* meets *Alien*’s corporate horror.) - **The Illusion of Choice:** Arthur is given "offers," but none of them are truly free. **Potential Plot Structures:** **Option 1: The Corporate Espionage Thriller** - Arthur is caught between the MCMG, the FMC, and Antares, each offering him a deal. - He realizes Azra isn’t just a metal—it’s a *catalyst* that can destabilize entire planets if weaponized. - The Antares Envoy is actually a defector from a rival faction, and his "diplomatic" offer is a trap. - Arthur must decide: sell out, fight back, or destroy the vein entirely. **Option 2: The Cosmic Horror Twist** - Azra isn’t just a metal—it’s a remnant of an ancient alien process, possibly even a failed terraforming attempt. - Miners who work with it too long start experiencing "hallucinations"—or are they *memories* of something that was here before? - The Antares Envoy knows more than he’s letting on, and his faction has been *suppressing* Azra’s true nature. - Arthur must decide whether to expose the truth (risking panic) or bury it (risking worse consequences). **Option 3: The Political Chess Game** - Antares isn’t a government—it’s a *corporate state*, and they want Azra to tip the balance in an interstellar cold war. - Arthur is a pawn, but a valuable one—he’s the only one who can safely extract Azra (for reasons yet unknown). - The FMC and MCMG are both trying to recruit him, but neither can be trusted. - The climax involves Arthur playing both sides against each other, forcing a stalemate. **Option 4: The Heist Gone Wrong** - Arthur isn’t just a miner—he’s a former engineer for the MCMG who knows their security protocols. - The Antares Envoy offers him a deal: steal a sample of Azra from a high-security vault in exchange for his freedom. - But the vault is a trap—it’s not just Azra inside, but something *else*. - Arthur must escape with the sample, but now he’s a fugitive from *everyone*. --- ### **3. Tone & Style** - **Asimov’s Influence:** - **Big-Picture Worldbuilding:** The story should feel like it’s part of a larger universe, with hints of interstellar politics, corporate feudalism, and scientific mysteries. - **Dialogue-Driven:** Key scenes should rely on sharp, expository dialogue (like Asimov’s *The Caves of Steel* or *Foundation*). - **Retrofuturism:** Think 1950s sci-fi aesthetics—spaceships with analog controls, diplomats in tailored uniforms, miners in rugged, patched-up gear. - **Moral Dilemmas:** Arthur should be forced to make choices where there are no good options, only *less bad* ones. - **Pacing:** - **Slow Burn:** The first few chapters should establish Arthur’s world, the discovery, and the initial pressure. - **Mid-Story Escalation:** By Chapter 4-5, Arthur should be fully entangled in the conflict, with stakes rising. - **Climax:** A high-stakes confrontation where Arthur’s choices determine the fate of Mars (or more). --- ### **4. Chapter-by-Chapter Beats (Broad Strokes)** **Chapter 1: The Invitation** - Arthur is aboard the transfer vessel, watching Mars shrink in the viewport. - Flashbacks to the armed retinue arriving at his homestead—polite but unyielding. - The Antares Envoy introduces himself, makes an offer. Arthur is suspicious but intrigued. - *Ends with:* Arthur arriving at an orbital station, realizing he’s in way over his head. **Chapter 2: The Offer** - Arthur meets Commander Voss in a sleek, retrofuturistic office with a view of Mars. - Voss explains Azra’s value—it’s not just a metal, it’s a *game-changer* for energy and weapons. - He offers Arthur a deal: exclusive rights to mine his claim, under Antares’ protection. - Arthur pushes back—what’s the catch? Voss smiles. *"There’s always a catch."* - *Ends with:* Arthur being shown a demonstration of Azra’s power—a small sample accelerating decay in a radioactive isotope, releasing a burst of energy. **Chapter 3: The Other Players** - Arthur is given a tour of the station, where he sees representatives from the MCMG and the FMC. - A smuggler (Captain Rivas) slips him a message: *"They’re not telling you the whole story."* - Arthur sneaks into a restricted area and finds Dr. Zhao, who warns him: *"Azra doesn’t just accelerate decay. It *rewrites* it."* - *Ends with:* Arthur being caught by security—Voss is waiting. *"I was hoping you’d be curious."* **Chapter 4: The Truth About Azra** - Voss reveals that Azra isn’t just valuable—it’s *dangerous*. The first miners who found it went mad, or worse. - He claims Antares is the only faction that can *control* it. The MCMG and FMC would weaponize it. - Arthur is given a choice: sign the deal, or be "detained for his own safety." - *Ends with:* Arthur stalling—he needs time to think. **Chapter 5: The Escape** - Arthur fakes compliance, but Rivas helps him escape the station. - They steal a ship and head back to Mars, but the MCMG is already moving in on his claim. - Dr. Zhao contacts him: *"You can’t run. They’ll find you. But there’s another way."* - *Ends with:* Arthur realizing he’s being hunted by *everyone*. **Chapter 6: The Martian Underground** - Arthur goes into hiding with the FMC, who offer him a different deal: help them steal Azra from the MCMG, and they’ll give him his freedom. - He learns that Azra isn’t just a metal—it’s *alive* in some way. The miners who went mad? They didn’t just hallucinate. They *remembered*. - *Ends with:* The FMC’s plan is set in motion—Arthur must infiltrate an MCMG facility. **Chapter 7: The Heist** - Arthur and a team of FMC operatives break into the MCMG vault. - They find Azra—but also something else: a *machine*, or a *being*, buried beneath the vein. - The MCMG ambushes them. Rivas is killed. Arthur barely escapes with a sample. - *Ends with:* Voss contacts him one last time: *"You have something that doesn’t belong to you."* **Chapter 8: The Gambit** - Arthur realizes the only way to stop the war over Azra is to *destroy it*—but that might unleash something worse. - He devises a plan: use the sample to trigger a controlled reaction, making the vein unstable. - Final confrontation with Voss, who reveals his true goal: he doesn’t want to control Azra—he wants to *awaken* it. - Arthur triggers the reaction. The vein collapses. Voss is consumed by the energy. - *Ending Options:* - **Bittersweet:** Arthur survives, but Mars is now a battleground. The MCMG and FMC blame each other. He’s a fugitive, but free. - **Hopeful:** The destruction of the vein scares off the factions. Arthur and the remaining miners form a new collective. - **Ambiguous:** The energy from the collapse doesn’t dissipate—it *spreads*. The story ends with a radio transmission from Earth: *"What have you done?"* --- ## **# Reflection** ### **What Works?** 1. **Arthur as a Relatable Protagonist:** - He’s not a hero or a villain—he’s a working man caught in forces beyond his control. This makes him easy to root for. - His arc from "just trying to survive" to "making hard choices" fits Asimov’s style of grounded, human-scale sci-fi. 2. **The Azra Mystery:** - The idea that Azra isn’t just a MacGuffin but has *hidden properties* (cosmic horror, sentience, or a scientific anomaly) adds depth. - It allows for both hard sci-fi (radioactive decay acceleration) and speculative elements (what if it’s *alive*?). 3. **Corporate & Political Intrigue:** - The conflict between the MCMG, FMC, and Antares creates a *Foundation*-like web of factions, each with their own agendas. - The retrofuturistic aesthetic (diplomats in uniforms, analog tech, corporate feudalism) gives it a classic sci-fi feel. 4. **Moral Ambiguity:** - There are no clear "good guys." Even the FMC, who seem like underdogs, have their own ruthless methods. - Arthur’s choices should feel *real*—no perfect solutions, only trade-offs. 5. **Pacing & Structure:** - The 8-chapter plan allows for a slow burn (Ch. 1-3), escalation (Ch. 4-6), and a high-stakes climax (Ch. 7-8). - Each chapter can end on a hook, keeping the reader engaged. --- ### **What Needs Refinement?** 1. **The Antares Envoy’s Role:** - Is he a true antagonist, or is he just another player in a larger game? - If he’s a defector or a rogue, his motives need to be clearer. If he’s just a corporate stooge, he might feel one-dimensional. - *Possible Fix:* Make him a *believer*—he thinks Azra is the key to "saving humanity," even if it means burning Mars to do it. 2. **The Azra Mystery’s Payoff:** - If Azra is just a super-metal, the story risks feeling like a standard corporate thriller. - If it’s *alive* or *alien*, the reveal needs to be earned—otherwise, it feels like a cheap twist. - *Possible Fix:* Foreshadow early (e.g., Arthur notices strange readings, miners talk about "voices" in the tunnels). 3. **Arthur’s Agency:** - He starts as a passive character (being dragged into events). He needs to *act* sooner rather than later. - *Possible Fix:* Give him a personal stake early—maybe his sister is an indentured laborer, or he has a debt to the MCMG. 4. **The Ending’s Stakes:** - If the ending is too small (Arthur just escapes), it might feel anticlimactic. - If it’s too big (Azra destroys Mars), it might feel unearned. - *Possible Fix:* The ending should tie into Arthur’s personal arc—does he save his community? Does he become a fugitive? Does he have to live with the consequences of his choices? 5. **The Retrofuturistic Aesthetic:** - Asimov’s sci-fi often had a "used future" feel—technology that was advanced but not sleek, societies that were bureaucratic and flawed. - *Possible Fix:* Describe the Antares station as *too* clean, *too* polished—hinting at something sinister beneath the surface. The MCMG’s facilities should feel like a dystopian corporate hellscape. --- ### **Final Thoughts: What’s the Best Direction?** After brainstorming, **Option 3 (The Political Chess Game) with elements of Option 2 (The Cosmic Horror Twist)** seems the strongest. Here’s why: - **It keeps the Asimovian focus on ideas and politics** (corporate states, cold wars, moral dilemmas) while adding a layer of mystery. - **It allows for Arthur to be a pawn who becomes a player**, which is a satisfying arc. - **The Azra mystery can be revealed gradually**, making the story feel like a slow unraveling of a larger truth. - **The ending can be ambiguous but meaningful**—Arthur doesn’t "win," but he changes the game. **Potential Title Tweaks:** - *The Azra Gambit* (current) is strong, but could also be: - *The Decay Accelerant* - *The Mars Vein* - *The Antares Offer* - *The Weight of Azra* --- ## **Next Steps: Finalizing the Outline** Before writing, I’d refine: 1. **Arthur’s personal stakes** (family? debt? a past with the MCMG?). 2. **Voss’s true motives** (is he a true believer? a corporate zealot? a rogue scientist?). 3. **The Azra reveal** (how much is science, how much is speculation?). 4. **The ending’s tone** (bittersweet? hopeful? ominous?). Would you like me to proceed with a **detailed chapter-by-chapter outline** based on this reflection?
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# **Intention** ### **Core Themes & Story Goals** 1. **A Human Story in a Cosmic Setting** - *Aim for:* Arthur Neegan as an everyman—pragmatic, flawed, but fundamentally decent—caught in forces beyond his control. His struggle should feel personal, not just a political chess piece. - *Avoid:* Making him a passive victim or an action hero. He should be out of his depth but resourceful in small, human ways (e.g., using his mining knowledge to outthink opponents, not laser guns). 2. **Corporate Colonialism as the Villain** - *Aim for:* A *Foundation*-esque critique of unchecked corporate power, where "diplomacy" is just another tool of exploitation. Mars should feel like a company town, not a frontier. - *Avoid:* Mustache-twirling villains. The MCMG, Antares, and the FMC should all believe they’re doing the "right" thing—just with different definitions of "right." 3. **Azra as a MacGuffin with Teeth** - *Aim for:* Azra isn’t just a valuable metal—it’s a *scientific anomaly* with unsettling implications. Its properties should hint at something larger (e.g., alien tech, a failed terraforming experiment, or a sentient force). - *Avoid:* Over-explaining. Let the mystery linger. Think *The Thing* (1982) or *Annihilation*—the less Arthur (and the reader) understand, the more unsettling it becomes. 4. **Asimovian Dialogue & Worldbuilding** - *Aim for:* - **Expository dialogue** that feels natural (e.g., characters debating Azra’s properties while negotiating, not monologuing). - **Retrofuturistic aesthetics**: Analog tech, bureaucratic jargon, and a sense of a lived-in solar system (e.g., Mars as a backwater, Earth as a distant overlord). - **Big-picture stakes** (interstellar politics) contrasted with **small-scale human drama** (Arthur’s survival, his relationships). - *Avoid:* - Info-dumps. Reveal worldbuilding through conflict (e.g., a corporate rep and a smuggler arguing over jurisdiction). - Overly modern tech. No holograms or AI—think *Blade Runner*’s retrofuturism or *The Expanse*’s grounded realism. 5. **Moral Ambiguity & Hard Choices** - *Aim for:* No clear "good guys." The FMC are underdogs but violent. Antares is polished but ruthless. The MCMG is the devil Arthur knows. - *Avoid:* Easy solutions. Arthur’s choices should have consequences, and the ending should feel earned, not tidy. 6. **Pacing & Structure** - *Aim for:* - **Chapters 1–3:** Slow burn. Establish Arthur’s world, the discovery, and the initial pressure. - **Chapters 4–6:** Escalation. Arthur is pulled deeper into the conflict, learning more about Azra’s secrets. - **Chapters 7–8:** Climax and resolution. A high-stakes confrontation where Arthur’s choices determine the outcome. - *Avoid:* Rushing the mystery. Let the tension build organically. --- ### **Stylistic Choices** - **Narrative Voice:** - **Third-person limited** (Arthur’s POV). This keeps the focus on his perspective and limits the reader’s knowledge to what he knows. - **Dry, understated tone**—Arthur is a miner, not a poet. His observations should be pragmatic, with flashes of dark humor. - **Minimal internal monologue.** Show his thoughts through action and dialogue (e.g., instead of "Arthur felt trapped," show him calculating escape routes). - **Dialogue:** - **Asimov-style:** Characters should sound like they’re debating ideas, not just bantering. Even threats should feel intellectual. - **Corporate jargon:** Terms like "indemnity clauses," "extraterritorial leases," and "decay acceleration quotas" should pepper conversations to reinforce the bureaucratic dystopia. - **Subtext:** No one says what they really mean. E.g., when Voss says, *"We’re offering you protection,"* he means, *"We’re offering you a cage."* - **Descriptions:** - **Sensory details:** Mars should feel *real*—the dust, the cold, the hum of machinery. The Antares station should feel *sterile* and *oppressive*. - **Retrofuturistic tech:** Spaceships with analog dials, mining rigs patched together with scavenged parts, diplomats in tailored uniforms with *too many* medals. - **Azra’s presence:** Describe it as *wrong*—not just glowing, but *pulsing*, *breathing*, or *whispering* in a way that makes Arthur’s skin crawl. --- ### **What to Avoid** 1. **Overpowered Protagonist:** Arthur is a mining engineer, not a soldier or a spy. His strengths should be his knowledge of Azra’s properties and his ability to navigate Martian society. 2. **Exposition Dumps:** No "As you know, Bob" dialogue. If a character explains something, it should be because they’re trying to *persuade* or *deceive* Arthur. 3. **Clichéd Sci-Fi Tropes:** - No "chosen one" narratives. - No last-minute techno-fixes (e.g., "I’ll just reverse the polarity!"). - No aliens (unless they’re *very* subtle—Azra’s mystery should feel scientific, not fantastical). 4. **Neat Endings:** The story should feel *resolved* but not *tidy*. Arthur’s choices should have consequences, and the fate of Azra should linger. --- # **Chapter Planning** ### **Chapter 1: The Invitation** **Title:** *"You’ve Been Selected"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Establish Arthur’s world, the discovery of Azra, and the inciting incident (the "invitation"). **Key Beats:** - **Opening:** Arthur aboard a transfer vessel, watching Mars shrink in the viewport. He’s uneasy—this isn’t a trip he chose. - **Flashback:** The armed retinue arrives at his homestead. They’re polite but unyielding, wearing Antares insignia. Their leader, *Special Attaché Voss*, offers Arthur a "once-in-a-lifetime opportunity." - **Arthur’s Resistance:** He refuses at first, but they make it clear this isn’t optional. *"We’re not asking, Mr. Neegan. We’re inviting you to a meeting where you’ll be given a choice."* - **The Transfer:** Arthur is taken to a ship. The crew ignores his questions. He notices the ship’s design—*too* sleek, *too* clean. It feels like a gilded cage. - **Arrival:** The ship docks at an orbital station. Voss greets him with a smile. *"Welcome to the Antares Diplomatic Corps, Mr. Neegan. We have much to discuss."* **Ending Hook:** Arthur is led into a conference room. On the table: a sample of Azra, pulsing faintly under the lights. --- ### **Chapter 2: The Offer** **Title:** *"The Weight of a Vein"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Introduce the stakes, Voss’s charm, and the first hints of Azra’s true nature. **Key Beats:** - **The Meeting:** Voss explains Azra’s properties—how it accelerates radioactive decay, making it invaluable for energy and weapons. *"It’s the most important discovery since fission."* - **The Offer:** Antares will give Arthur exclusive rights to mine his claim, plus a cut of the profits. In exchange, he signs a "non-disclosure and cooperation agreement." - **Arthur’s Suspicion:** He pushes back. *"What’s the catch?"* Voss smiles. *"There’s always a catch. But this one’s in your favor."* - **The Demonstration:** Voss places a sample of Azra near a radioactive isotope. The decay accelerates visibly—energy crackles, the isotope glows brighter. *"Imagine what this could do for Mars."* - **The Warning:** Voss casually mentions that the first miners who found Azra "didn’t handle it properly." Some went mad. Others *disappeared*. - **The Ultimatum:** Voss gives Arthur time to think—but not too much. *"The MCMG is already moving on your claim. You have until we reach the station to decide."* **Ending Hook:** Arthur is left alone with the Azra sample. It *hums*. He swears it’s watching him. --- ### **Chapter 3: The Other Players** **Title:** *"The Station"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Introduce the MCMG and FMC, and the first hints that Azra is more than it seems. **Key Beats:** - **The Tour:** A junior attaché gives Arthur a tour of the station. He sees MCMG reps in corporate gray, FMC operatives in patched-up gear. Neither group looks friendly. - **The Smuggler:** A rough-looking woman (Captain Rivas) slips him a note: *"They’re lying. Meet me in Dock 12."* - **The Restricted Area:** Arthur sneaks into a lab. He finds *Dr. Lien Zhao*, a physicist in a cell. She’s been studying Azra. *"It’s not just a metal. It’s a *catalyst*. And it’s *hungry*."* - **The Chase:** Security catches him. Voss is waiting. *"Curiosity is a dangerous trait, Mr. Neegan."* - **The Choice:** Voss gives him one last chance to sign. *"Or we can detain you for your own safety. Your call."* **Ending Hook:** Arthur signs the agreement—but only to buy time. As he’s escorted back to his quarters, he notices the station’s air filters are *failing*. --- ### **Chapter 4: The Truth About Azra** **Title:** *"The First Miners"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Reveal Azra’s true dangers and Arthur’s growing paranoia. **Key Beats:** - **The Briefing:** Voss takes Arthur to a secure room. He shows footage of the first Azra miners—their faces *melting*, their voices *layered*, as if speaking in multiple timelines at once. - **The Explanation:** Voss admits Azra doesn’t just accelerate decay—it *rewrites* it. *"It’s not just a metal. It’s a *process*."* - **The Justification:** Antares wants to control it to prevent a war. The MCMG and FMC would weaponize it. *"We’re the only ones who can handle it responsibly."* - **Arthur’s Realization:** He’s not a partner—he’s a *tool*. Antares needs him because his claim is the only one with a *stable* Azra vein. *"You’re not offering me a deal. You’re offering me a leash."* - **The Escape Plan:** Arthur pretends to comply. That night, he contacts Rivas. *"I need to get off this station."* **Ending Hook:** Rivas smirks. *"Good. Because the MCMG just raided your claim. You’ve got nothing left to go back to."* --- ### **Chapter 5: The Escape** **Title:** *"No Way Home"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Arthur flees the station, but his problems are just beginning. **Key Beats:** - **The Heist:** Rivas helps Arthur steal a ship. They barely escape as Antares security gives chase. - **The Betrayal:** Rivas reveals she’s FMC. *"We can help you. But you’ll owe us."* - **The Return to Mars:** Arthur’s homestead is a smoldering ruin. The MCMG has already moved in. His neighbors are gone—some dead, some indentured. - **The Offer:** The FMC’s leader, *Kael*, offers Arthur a deal: help them steal Azra from the MCMG, and they’ll give him his claim back. *"Or you can run. But they’ll find you."* - **The Decision:** Arthur agrees—but only because he has no other choice. *"I’m not fighting for you. I’m fighting for my home."* **Ending Hook:** Kael smiles. *"Smart man. But you should know—Azra doesn’t like to be stolen."* --- ### **Chapter 6: The Martian Underground** **Title:** *"The Vein"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Arthur learns the full extent of Azra’s mystery and the FMC’s true motives. **Key Beats:** - **The Briefing:** The FMC shows Arthur their intel. The MCMG isn’t just mining Azra—they’re *experimenting* on it. *"They’re trying to make it *stable*."* - **The Warning:** Dr. Zhao (now working with the FMC) warns him. *"Azra isn’t just a metal. It’s a *memory*. It remembers what was here before Mars."* - **The Plan:** The FMC wants to destroy the MCMG’s facility and steal a sample. Arthur’s job is to get them inside. *"You’re the only one who knows how to handle it."* - **The Doubt:** Arthur starts to wonder if the FMC are any better than Antares or the MCMG. *"You’re not fighting for Mars. You’re fighting for control."* - **The Vision:** While prepping for the heist, Arthur touches Azra. For a second, he *sees* something—a city, a civilization, *under* the dust. Then it’s gone. **Ending Hook:** Kael hands him a weapon. *"Tonight, we take back Mars."* --- ### **Chapter 7: The Heist** **Title:** *"The Machine"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** The mission goes wrong, and Arthur discovers Azra’s final secret. **Key Beats:** - **The Infiltration:** Arthur and the FMC team break into the MCMG facility. The place is a nightmare—miners in cages, Azra samples pulsing in tanks. - **The Discovery:** They find the *machine*—a massive, alien-looking device buried beneath the vein. It’s *reacting* to Azra. - **The Betrayal:** The MCMG ambushes them. Rivas is killed. Kael is captured. Arthur barely escapes with a sample. - **The Truth:** The machine isn’t human. It’s *old*. And it’s *awake*. - **The Choice:** Arthur realizes the only way to stop the war is to *destroy* the vein—but that might activate the machine. **Ending Hook:** Voss’s voice crackles over the comms. *"Mr. Neegan. You have something that doesn’t belong to you."* --- ### **Chapter 8: The Gambit** **Title:** *"The Weight of Mars"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Arthur’s final choice and the story’s resolution. **Key Beats:** - **The Confrontation:** Voss corners Arthur. *"You don’t understand what you’re holding. Azra isn’t a weapon. It’s a *key*."* - **The Revelation:** Voss wants to *activate* the machine. *"It’s not a threat. It’s an *opportunity*. Mars can be more than a mining colony."* - **The Sacrifice:** Arthur realizes the machine isn’t just a device—it’s a *being*. And it’s *hungry*. - **The Decision:** Arthur triggers the Azra sample, causing a chain reaction. The vein collapses. The machine *screams*. - **The Aftermath:** - Voss is consumed by the energy. - The MCMG and FMC blame each other. Mars is plunged into chaos. - Arthur survives, but his claim is gone. He’s a fugitive. - **The Ending:** - **Option 1 (Bittersweet):** Arthur escapes with a handful of settlers. They form a new collective. The last shot is of the machine’s ruins, still *pulsing*. - **Option 2 (Ambiguous):** Arthur is picked up by a ship. The captain asks, *"What happened down there?"* Arthur looks at his hands—*they’re glowing*. *"Nothing good."* - **Option 3 (Hopeful):** The destruction of the vein scares off the factions. Arthur and the remaining miners rebuild. The last line: *"Mars wasn’t free. But it was theirs."* **Final Hook:** A transmission from Earth: *"Unidentified energy surge detected on Mars. Investigate immediately."* The screen cuts to static. --- ### **Final Notes** - **Tone:** The story should feel like a cross between *Foundation* (political intrigue) and *Alien* (corporate horror), with a dash of *The Thing*’s paranoia. - **Arthur’s Arc:** From a man who just wants to mine in peace to someone who must decide the fate of Mars. His growth should be in his *agency*—he starts as a pawn, but by the end, he’s making the hard choices. - **Azra’s Role:** It should feel like a *character*—unpredictable, dangerous, and *alive* in some way. The less it’s explained, the better. Would you like any refinements to the chapter outlines or additional details on specific scenes?
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# **How a Human Writer Might Approach *The Azra Gambit*** Writing *The Azra Gambit* as a human—especially in the style of Asimov—requires a deep understanding of **character vulnerability, moral ambiguity, and the messiness of real-world stakes**. Humans write with **doubt, inconsistency, and emotional rawness**, while LLMs often default to **neatness, predictability, and performative cleverness**. Below, I’ll break down: 1. **Common LLM Writing Pitfalls** (and how they might manifest in this story). 2. **How a Human Writer Would Approach This Piece** (focusing on authenticity, imperfection, and emotional truth). 3. **A Deep Dive on the Current Plan’s Potential LLM Traps** (and how to avoid them). 4. **Brainstorming Ideas to Make It More Human**. --- ## **1. Common LLM Writing Pitfalls (and How They Might Appear Here)** ### **A. The "Safe" Story** **LLM Tendency:** Avoiding real risk—emotional, narrative, or thematic. Stories feel **sterile, inoffensive, and predictable**. **How It Might Manifest in *The Azra Gambit*:** - Arthur’s reactions are **too measured, too rational**. He never panics, never lashes out, never makes a *stupid* but *human* mistake. - The corporate villains are **cartoonishly evil** (e.g., twirling mustaches, monologuing about profit). In reality, corporate power is **banal, bureaucratic, and insidious**. - The ending is **too neat**—Arthur either "wins" or "loses" in a way that feels **preordained**, not earned. **Human Fix:** - Let Arthur **fail**. Let him make a bad call out of fear or pride. - Make the villains **believable**—they should think they’re the heroes (e.g., Voss genuinely believes Antares is saving humanity). - End the story in a way that **feels true to the characters**, not the plot. --- ### **B. The "Bow-Wrapped" Ending** **LLM Tendency:** Resolving all conflicts in a **tidy, emotionally satisfying** way. No loose ends, no ambiguity. **How It Might Manifest in *The Azra Gambit*:** - The Azra mystery is **fully explained** (e.g., "It was an alien terraforming device all along!"). - Arthur’s personal stakes are **resolved** (e.g., he gets his claim back, reunites with his sister, etc.). - The political conflict is **decided** (e.g., the FMC wins, Antares is defeated, Mars is free). **Human Fix:** - **Leave some mysteries unsolved.** Azra’s true nature should be **hinted at, not revealed**. - **Let the personal stakes linger.** Maybe Arthur’s sister is still indentured. Maybe he’s now a fugitive. **Not every thread needs to be tied.** - **The political conflict should feel ongoing.** The story ends, but the war over Azra doesn’t—because **real-world conflicts don’t wrap up neatly**. --- ### **C. Performative Cleverness Over Readability** **LLM Tendency:** Prioritizing **complexity, wordplay, or "impressive" prose** over **clarity and emotional impact**. **How It Might Manifest in *The Azra Gambit*:** - **Overly elaborate descriptions** (e.g., "The Antares station was a monolith of obsidian alloy, its geometric precision a testament to the cold calculus of interstellar diplomacy" → **too much, too abstract**). - **Dialogue that sounds like a debate club** (e.g., characters **explaining their philosophies** instead of **arguing like real people**). - **Jargon-heavy exposition** (e.g., dumping Azra’s scientific properties in a **dense, unnatural** info-dump). **Human Fix:** - **Describe like a miner, not a poet.** Arthur should notice **dust in the air, the hum of machinery, the way Voss’s gloves creak when he flexes his fingers**—not "the existential dread of corporate hegemony." - **Dialogue should sound like people, not manifestos.** Even smart people **interrupt, stutter, lie, and change the subject**. - **Exposition should feel organic.** If a character explains something, it should be because they’re **trying to persuade, deceive, or warn**—not because the plot demands it. --- ### **D. The "Plot-Driven" Story (Instead of Character-Driven)** **LLM Tendency:** Treating characters as **vehicles for the plot** rather than **the heart of the story**. **How It Might Manifest in *The Azra Gambit*:** - Arthur’s **only role is to react to events** (e.g., he’s kidnapped → he escapes → he joins the FMC → he steals Azra). He **doesn’t drive the story**. - His **personal life is an afterthought** (e.g., his sister is mentioned once, then forgotten). - His **choices don’t have real consequences** (e.g., he betrays the FMC, but it doesn’t matter because the plot moves on). **Human Fix:** - **Give Arthur agency.** He should **make choices that shape the story**, even if they’re bad choices. - **Make his personal life matter.** If he has a sister, **show her in danger**. If he owes a debt, **make him pay it**. - **Let his flaws hurt him.** If he’s stubborn, **let it get him captured**. If he’s distrustful, **let it ruin an alliance**. --- ### **E. The "Theme Over Character" Problem** **LLM Tendency:** Prioritizing **themes** (e.g., "corporate greed is bad") over **character arcs**. **How It Might Manifest in *The Azra Gambit*:** - Arthur **exists to illustrate the theme** (e.g., he’s just a stand-in for "the little guy vs. the system"). - The story **lectures the reader** (e.g., characters give speeches about colonialism instead of **showing it through action**). - The **ending is symbolic but hollow** (e.g., Arthur destroys Azra to "free Mars," but we don’t see the **human cost**). **Human Fix:** - **Themes should emerge from character.** If the story is about exploitation, **show Arthur being exploited**—not just **tell** us. - **Avoid moralizing.** Let the reader **draw their own conclusions**. - **The ending should be about people, not ideas.** If Arthur destroys Azra, **show how it affects the miners, the corporations, his sister**—not just "Mars is free now." --- ## **2. How a Human Writer Would Approach This Piece** ### **A. Start with Arthur’s Humanity** A human writer would **ground the story in Arthur’s emotional reality** before diving into sci-fi intrigue. **How?** - **Open with a small, personal moment.** Maybe Arthur is **fixing a broken drill** when the Antares retinue arrives. Maybe he’s **writing a letter to his sister** (who’s indentured to the MCMG). - **Show his daily life.** What does he eat? What does he fear? What does he **pretend not to care about**? - **Make his reactions messy.** When Voss offers him a deal, he should **hesitate, bargain, get angry, then backtrack**—not just "hmm, interesting." **Example of a Human Opening:** > *Arthur Neegan had been patching the same drill for three hours when the knock came. Not the usual knock—the kind that meant trouble. He wiped grease on his pants, left the drill on the workbench, and opened the door to four men in red-starred coats. The one in front smiled. "Mr. Neegan. We’d like to discuss an opportunity." Arthur’s first thought wasn’t about Azra or Mars or the MCMG. It was: *They know about the still.* --- ### **B. Make the Corporations Feel Real** LLMs often write corporations as **monolithic, faceless entities**. Humans know that **corporations are made of people**—some greedy, some idealistic, some just doing their jobs. **How?** - **Show the bureaucracy.** Maybe Arthur has to **fill out forms in triplicate** to get his claim registered. Maybe a low-level MCMG clerk **takes a bribe to look the other way**. - **Give the villains human motives.** Voss shouldn’t just want power—he should **believe he’s saving humanity**. The MCMG CEO shouldn’t be a mustache-twirler—he should be a **tired old man who thinks he’s doing what’s best for Mars**. - **Show the cost of corporate rule.** Maybe Arthur’s neighbor **disappeared after complaining about safety violations**. Maybe the FMC’s leader is **a former MCMG engineer who saw too much**. **Example of Human Corporate Worldbuilding:** > *The MCMG office on Mars was a squat, windowless building near the spaceport. Arthur had been here before—once, when he registered his claim, and again when they fined him for "unauthorized equipment modifications." The clerk behind the desk didn’t look up. "Name?" "Neegan. Arthur Neegan." The clerk tapped at his terminal. "Ah. You’re the one with the Azra anomaly." Arthur’s stomach twisted. "It’s not an anomaly. It’s a vein." The clerk finally met his eyes. "That’s not what the report says."* --- ### **C. Let the Science Be Messy** LLMs love **neat, explainable sci-fi**. Humans know that **real science is confusing, contradictory, and often wrong**. **How?** - **Don’t fully explain Azra.** Let Arthur (and the reader) **piece it together from fragments**—rumors, half-remembered warnings, eerie observations. - **Make the experts disagree.** Dr. Zhao thinks Azra is **alive**. The MCMG scientists think it’s **just a metal**. The FMC thinks it’s **a weapon**. - **Show the human cost of the science.** Maybe miners who work with Azra **start seeing things**. Maybe their **bones ache**. Maybe they **don’t age right**. **Example of Messy Science:** > *Dr. Zhao’s hands shook as she adjusted the scanner. "It’s not just accelerating decay. It’s *rewriting* it. Like it’s… editing the universe." Arthur frowned. "That’s not possible." "Neither is a metal that glows in the dark. But here we are." She tapped the screen. The readings made no sense—half the numbers were negative. "I think it’s *learning*."* --- ### **D. Make the Dialogue Feel Lived-In** LLM dialogue often sounds like **a debate or a negotiation**. Human dialogue is **messy, interrupted, and full of subtext**. **How?** - **Characters should lie, evade, and change the subject.** Voss shouldn’t **explain his plan**—he should **hint at it, then pivot to small talk**. - **Let them argue about stupid things.** Maybe Arthur and Rivas **bicker about the best way to smuggle Azra**. Maybe Voss **complains about Martian food**. - **Use silence.** Not every line needs a response. Sometimes, **a character just stares**. **Example of Human Dialogue:** > *"You’re not telling me everything," Arthur said. Voss sipped his tea. "No." "Why?" "Because you wouldn’t believe me." Arthur laughed. "Try me." Voss set the cup down. "Azra isn’t a metal. It’s a *memory*." A long silence. Arthur finally said, "That’s the dumbest thing I’ve ever heard." Voss smiled. "I know."* --- ### **E. Let the Ending Be Uncomfortable** LLMs love **neat resolutions**. Humans know that **life doesn’t wrap up with a bow**. **How?** - **Don’t resolve Arthur’s personal stakes.** Maybe his sister is still indentured. Maybe he’s now a fugitive. **Not everything gets fixed.** - **Don’t fully explain Azra.** Let the mystery **linger**. Maybe the machine is still active. Maybe Arthur starts **hearing things**. - **Don’t pick a "winner."** The MCMG, Antares, and the FMC should all **lose something**, but the conflict should **continue**. **Example of a Human Ending:** > *The vein collapsed in on itself, taking the machine with it. Arthur didn’t wait to see if it worked. He ran. By the time he reached the surface, the MCMG was already blaming the FMC, and the FMC was blaming Antares. No one mentioned Arthur. No one mentioned the miners who’d died in the collapse. He found Rivas’s body half-buried in the dust. Her eyes were open. He closed them. Then he kept walking. Somewhere out there, his sister was still working off her debt. Somewhere out there, Voss was still alive. Somewhere out there, Azra was still waiting.* --- ## **3. Deep Dive: Critiquing the Current Plan for LLM Tropes** ### **A. Arthur’s Agency (or Lack Thereof)** **Potential LLM Trap:** - Arthur is **reactive** for most of the story. He’s kidnapped, given offers, escapes, joins the FMC—**but he’s not driving the plot**. - His **personal stakes are vague**. We know he has a claim, but we don’t know **why it matters to him**. **Human Fix Ideas:** - **Give him a concrete personal stake.** Maybe his sister is **indentured to the MCMG**, and he’s been trying to buy her freedom. Maybe he **owes a debt** to a smuggler. - **Let him make a bad choice early.** Maybe he **sells a small sample of Azra** to a middleman, and it **gets someone killed**. Now he’s **complicit**. - **Make him proactive.** Instead of just **reacting to Voss’s offer**, maybe he **tries to sell Azra himself**—and that’s how he gets noticed. --- ### **B. The Corporations Are Too Monolithic** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The MCMG, Antares, and the FMC are **faceless entities**. We don’t see the **people** who make them up. - Their motives are **too simple** (e.g., "Antares wants to control Azra for power"). **Human Fix Ideas:** - **Show the bureaucracy.** Maybe Arthur has to **navigate a corrupt MCMG office** to register his claim. Maybe a **low-level clerk** takes a bribe to help him. - **Give the villains human motives.** Voss shouldn’t just want power—he should **believe he’s saving humanity**. The MCMG CEO should be a **tired old man** who thinks he’s doing what’s best for Mars. - **Show the cost of corporate rule.** Maybe Arthur’s neighbor **disappeared after complaining about safety violations**. Maybe the FMC’s leader is a **former MCMG engineer who saw too much**. --- ### **C. The Azra Mystery Is Too Neat** **Potential LLM Trap:** - Azra’s properties are **explained too clearly** (e.g., "It accelerates decay and rewrites it"). - The **cosmic horror element** is **revealed too late** (Chapter 6), making it feel like an **afterthought**. **Human Fix Ideas:** - **Foreshadow early.** Maybe Arthur **notices strange readings** in Chapter 1. Maybe miners **whisper about "the voices"** in the tunnels. - **Let the mystery linger.** Don’t **fully explain** Azra. Let Arthur (and the reader) **piece it together from fragments**. - **Make the science messy.** Maybe Dr. Zhao **disagrees with the MCMG scientists**. Maybe the FMC thinks it’s a **weapon**, not a metal. --- ### **D. The Dialogue Is Too "On the Nose"** **Potential LLM Trap:** - Characters **explain their motives** instead of **hinting at them**. - Dialogue feels like a **negotiation or a debate**, not a **conversation**. **Human Fix Ideas:** - **Let characters lie.** Voss shouldn’t **explain his plan**—he should **hint at it, then change the subject**. - **Let them argue about stupid things.** Maybe Arthur and Rivas **bicker about the best way to smuggle Azra**. Maybe Voss **complains about Martian food**. - **Use silence.** Not every line needs a response. Sometimes, **a character just stares**. --- ### **E. The Ending Is Too Tidy** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The ending **resolves all conflicts** (e.g., Arthur destroys Azra, Mars is free, the factions are defeated). - The **personal stakes are wrapped up** (e.g., Arthur gets his claim back, reunites with his sister). **Human Fix Ideas:** - **Don’t resolve Arthur’s personal stakes.** Maybe his sister is still indentured. Maybe he’s now a fugitive. - **Don’t fully explain Azra.** Let the mystery **linger**. Maybe the machine is still active. Maybe Arthur starts **hearing things**. - **Don’t pick a "winner."** The MCMG, Antares, and the FMC should all **lose something**, but the conflict should **continue**. --- ## **4. Brainstorming Ideas to Make It More Human** ### **A. Arthur’s Flaws Should Hurt Him** **Idea 1: Stubbornness Gets Him Captured** - Arthur **refuses to trust the FMC** at first, even when they’re his only allies. - His **distrust gets him captured** by the MCMG, forcing him to **negotiate his way out**. **Idea 2: He Makes a Bad Deal** - Early on, Arthur **sells a small sample of Azra** to a smuggler to pay off a debt. - Later, he finds out that **sample was used to kill someone**. Now he’s **complicit**. **Idea 3: He’s Not as Smart as He Thinks** - Arthur **overestimates his knowledge of Azra**. - He **misjudges a reaction**, causing an accident that **kills a miner**. --- ### **B. The Corporations Should Feel Real** **Idea 1: The Bureaucracy Is the Real Enemy** - Arthur has to **navigate a corrupt MCMG office** to register his claim. - A **low-level clerk** takes a bribe to help him, but **reports him anyway**. **Idea 2: The Villains Are Just Doing Their Jobs** - Voss isn’t a mustache-twirler—he’s a **true believer** who thinks Antares is saving humanity. - The MCMG CEO is a **tired old man** who thinks he’s doing what’s best for Mars. **Idea 3: The Cost of Corporate Rule** - Arthur’s neighbor **disappeared after complaining about safety violations**. - The FMC’s leader is a **former MCMG engineer who saw too much**. --- ### **C. The Azra Mystery Should Be Unsettling** **Idea 1: Foreshadow Early** - In Chapter 1, Arthur **notices strange readings** in his mine. - Miners **whisper about "the voices"** in the tunnels. **Idea 2: Let the Mystery Linger** - Don’t **fully explain** Azra. Let Arthur (and the reader) **piece it together from fragments**. - Maybe Dr. Zhao **disagrees with the MCMG scientists**. Maybe the FMC thinks it’s a **weapon**, not a metal. **Idea 3: Make the Science Messy** - Azra’s properties **change depending on who’s studying it**. - Miners who work with it **start seeing things**. Their **bones ache**. They **don’t age right**. --- ### **D. The Dialogue Should Feel Lived-In** **Idea 1: Let Characters Lie** - Voss **hints at his plan**, then **changes the subject**. - Arthur **pretends to agree** with the FMC, but **secretly plans to betray them**. **Idea 2: Let Them Argue About Stupid Things** - Arthur and Rivas **bicker about the best way to smuggle Azra**. - Voss **complains about Martian food**. **Idea 3: Use Silence** - Not every line needs a response. Sometimes, **a character just stares**. - Maybe Arthur **doesn’t answer a question**, and the other character **gets angry**. --- ### **E. The Ending Should Be Uncomfortable** **Idea 1: Don’t Resolve Arthur’s Personal Stakes** - His sister is still **indentured to the MCMG**. - He’s now a **fugitive**, with no home to return to. **Idea 2: Don’t Fully Explain Azra** - The machine is still **active underground**. - Arthur starts **hearing things**—maybe it’s **calling to him**. **Idea 3: Don’t Pick a Winner** - The MCMG, Antares, and the FMC all **lose something**, but the conflict **continues**. - The story ends with a **radio transmission from Earth**: *"Unidentified energy surge detected on Mars. Investigate immediately."* --- ## **Final Thoughts: How to Make *The Azra Gambit* Feel Human** 1. **Start small.** Ground the story in **Arthur’s daily life** before diving into sci-fi intrigue. 2. **Make the corporations feel real.** Show the **bureaucracy, the people, the cost of their rule**. 3. **Let the science be messy.** Don’t **fully explain** Azra—let the mystery **linger**. 4. **Make the dialogue feel lived-in.** Let characters **lie, evade, and argue about stupid things**. 5. **End uncomfortably.** Don’t **wrap everything up**—let the **personal and political conflicts continue**. The key is to **remember that Arthur is a person, not a plot device**. He should **fail, hesitate, and make bad choices**—because that’s what **real people do**. Would you like to refine any of these ideas further before adjusting the chapter outlines?
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# **Final Plan for *The Azra Gambit*** *A Human-Like, Compelling Sci-Fi Short Story in 8 Chapters* --- # **Intention** ### **Core Themes & Story Goals** 1. **A Relatable, Flawed Protagonist** - *Aim for:* Arthur Neegan as a **working-class everyman**—stubborn, pragmatic, but not a hero. His motivations should be **personal and immediate** (e.g., debt, family, survival), not ideological. - *Avoid:* Making him overly clever, heroic, or passive. He should **make mistakes, hesitate, and act out of fear or pride**—not just plot convenience. 2. **Corporate Exploitation as a Living, Breathing Force** - *Aim for:* The MCMG, Antares, and FMC should feel like **real institutions**—bureaucratic, corrupt, and made of people who **believe they’re doing the right thing**. - *Avoid:* Mustache-twirling villains. The corporations should be **insidious, not cartoonish**—their power should feel **inescapable, not theatrical**. 3. **Azra as a Mystery, Not a MacGuffin** - *Aim for:* Azra should be **unsettling, not just valuable**. Its properties should **hint at something larger** (e.g., alien tech, a failed terraforming experiment, or a sentient force), but **never fully explained**. - *Avoid:* Over-explaining its origins or mechanics. The mystery should **linger**, making the reader (and Arthur) uneasy. 4. **Moral Ambiguity & Hard Choices** - *Aim for:* No clear "good guys." The FMC are underdogs but violent. Antares is polished but ruthless. The MCMG is the devil Arthur knows. - *Avoid:* Easy solutions. Arthur’s choices should have **consequences**, and the ending should feel **earned, not tidy**. 5. **Asimovian Dialogue & Worldbuilding** - *Aim for:* - **Expository dialogue that feels natural** (e.g., characters debating Azra’s properties while negotiating, not monologuing). - **Retrofuturistic aesthetics**: Analog tech, bureaucratic jargon, and a sense of a **lived-in solar system** (e.g., Mars as a backwater, Earth as a distant overlord). - **Big-picture stakes** (interstellar politics) contrasted with **small-scale human drama** (Arthur’s survival, his relationships). - *Avoid:* - Info-dumps. Reveal worldbuilding through **conflict** (e.g., a corporate rep and a smuggler arguing over jurisdiction). - Overly modern tech. No holograms or AI—think *Blade Runner*’s retrofuturism or *The Expanse*’s grounded realism. 6. **Pacing & Structure** - *Aim for:* - **Chapters 1–3:** Slow burn. Establish Arthur’s world, the discovery of Azra, and the initial pressure. - **Chapters 4–6:** Escalation. Arthur is pulled deeper into the conflict, learning more about Azra’s secrets. - **Chapters 7–8:** Climax and resolution. A high-stakes confrontation where Arthur’s choices determine the outcome. - *Avoid:* Rushing the mystery. Let the tension build **organically**. --- ### **Stylistic Choices** - **Narrative Voice:** - **Third-person limited** (Arthur’s POV). This keeps the focus on his perspective and limits the reader’s knowledge to what he knows. - **Dry, understated tone**—Arthur is a miner, not a poet. His observations should be **pragmatic, with flashes of dark humor**. - **Minimal internal monologue.** Show his thoughts through **action and dialogue** (e.g., instead of "Arthur felt trapped," show him calculating escape routes). - **Dialogue:** - **Asimov-style:** Characters should sound like they’re **debating ideas, not just bantering**. Even threats should feel **intellectual**. - **Corporate jargon:** Terms like "indemnity clauses," "extraterritorial leases," and "decay acceleration quotas" should pepper conversations to reinforce the **bureaucratic dystopia**. - **Subtext:** No one says what they really mean. E.g., when Voss says, *"We’re offering you protection,"* he means, *"We’re offering you a cage."* - **Descriptions:** - **Sensory details:** Mars should feel *real*—the dust, the cold, the hum of machinery. The Antares station should feel *sterile* and *oppressive*. - **Retrofuturistic tech:** Spaceships with analog dials, mining rigs patched together with scavenged parts, diplomats in tailored uniforms with *too many* medals. - **Azra’s presence:** Describe it as *wrong*—not just glowing, but *pulsing*, *breathing*, or *whispering* in a way that makes Arthur’s skin crawl. --- ### **What to Avoid** 1. **Overpowered Protagonist:** Arthur is a mining engineer, not a soldier or a spy. His strengths should be his **knowledge of Azra’s properties** and his ability to **navigate Martian society**, not combat skills. 2. **Exposition Dumps:** No "As you know, Bob" dialogue. If a character explains something, it should be because they’re **trying to persuade, deceive, or warn**—not because the plot demands it. 3. **Clichéd Sci-Fi Tropes:** - No "chosen one" narratives. - No last-minute techno-fixes (e.g., "I’ll just reverse the polarity!"). - No aliens (unless they’re *very* subtle—Azra’s mystery should feel **scientific, not fantastical**). 4. **Neat Endings:** The story should feel *resolved* but not *tidy*. Arthur’s choices should have **consequences**, and the fate of Azra should **linger**. --- # **Chapter Planning** ### **Chapter 1: The Knock at the Door** **Title:** *"The Wrong Kind of Visitors"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Establish Arthur’s world, his personal stakes, and the inciting incident (the "invitation"). **Key Beats:** - **Opening:** Arthur is **fixing a broken drill** in his homestead, cursing under his breath. He’s **behind on payments** to the MCMG, and his sister, **Lena**, is still indentured to them. - **The Knock:** Four armed men in **Antares uniforms** arrive. Their leader, *Special Attaché Voss*, is **polite but unyielding**. *"Mr. Neegan, we’d like to discuss an opportunity."* - **Arthur’s Resistance:** He refuses at first, but they make it clear this isn’t optional. *"We’re not asking. We’re inviting you to a meeting where you’ll be given a choice."* - **The Transfer:** Arthur is taken to a ship. The crew ignores his questions. He notices the ship’s design—*too* sleek, *too* clean. It feels like a **gilded cage**. - **Arrival:** The ship docks at an orbital station. Voss greets him with a smile. *"Welcome to the Antares Diplomatic Corps, Mr. Neegan. We have much to discuss."* - **The Hook:** Arthur is led into a conference room. On the table: a **sample of Azra**, pulsing faintly under the lights. It **hums**. **Ending Hook:** Arthur touches it. For a second, he **sees something**—a city, a civilization, *under* the dust. Then it’s gone. --- ### **Chapter 2: The Offer** **Title:** *"The Weight of a Vein"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Introduce the stakes, Voss’s charm, and the first hints of Azra’s true nature. **Key Beats:** - **The Meeting:** Voss explains Azra’s properties—how it **accelerates radioactive decay**, making it invaluable for energy and weapons. *"It’s the most important discovery since fission."* - **The Offer:** Antares will give Arthur **exclusive rights to mine his claim**, plus a cut of the profits. In exchange, he signs a **"non-disclosure and cooperation agreement."** - **Arthur’s Suspicion:** He pushes back. *"What’s the catch?"* Voss smiles. *"There’s always a catch. But this one’s in your favor."* - **The Demonstration:** Voss places a sample of Azra near a **radioactive isotope**. The decay accelerates visibly—energy crackles, the isotope glows brighter. *"Imagine what this could do for Mars."* - **The Warning:** Voss casually mentions that the first miners who found Azra **"didn’t handle it properly."** Some went mad. Others *disappeared*. - **The Ultimatum:** Voss gives Arthur time to think—but not too much. *"The MCMG is already moving on your claim. You have until we reach the station to decide."* **Ending Hook:** Arthur is left alone with the Azra sample. It **pulses in time with his heartbeat**. --- ### **Chapter 3: The Station** **Title:** *"The Wrong Kind of People"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Introduce the MCMG and FMC, and the first hints that Azra is more than it seems. **Key Beats:** - **The Tour:** A junior attaché gives Arthur a tour of the station. He sees **MCMG reps in corporate gray**, **FMC operatives in patched-up gear**. Neither group looks friendly. - **The Smuggler:** A rough-looking woman (**Captain Rivas**) slips him a note: *"They’re lying. Meet me in Dock 12."* - **The Restricted Area:** Arthur sneaks into a lab. He finds **Dr. Lien Zhao**, a physicist in a cell. She’s been studying Azra. *"It’s not just a metal. It’s a *catalyst*. And it’s *hungry*."* - **The Chase:** Security catches him. Voss is waiting. *"Curiosity is a dangerous trait, Mr. Neegan."* - **The Choice:** Voss gives him one last chance to sign. *"Or we can detain you for your own safety. Your call."* **Ending Hook:** Arthur signs the agreement—but only to buy time. As he’s escorted back to his quarters, he notices the station’s **air filters are failing**. --- ### **Chapter 4: The First Miners** **Title:** *"The Things They Saw"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Reveal Azra’s true dangers and Arthur’s growing paranoia. **Key Beats:** - **The Briefing:** Voss takes Arthur to a secure room. He shows footage of the first Azra miners—their faces *melting*, their voices *layered*, as if speaking in multiple timelines at once. - **The Explanation:** Voss admits Azra doesn’t just accelerate decay—it *rewrites* it. *"It’s not just a metal. It’s a *process*."* - **The Justification:** Antares wants to control it to prevent a war. The MCMG and FMC would weaponize it. *"We’re the only ones who can handle it responsibly."* - **Arthur’s Realization:** He’s not a partner—he’s a *tool*. Antares needs him because his claim is the only one with a *stable* Azra vein. *"You’re not offering me a deal. You’re offering me a leash."* - **The Escape Plan:** Arthur pretends to comply. That night, he contacts Rivas. *"I need to get off this station."* **Ending Hook:** Rivas smirks. *"Good. Because the MCMG just raided your claim. You’ve got nothing left to go back to."* --- ### **Chapter 5: No Way Home** **Title:** *"The Things They Took"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Arthur flees the station, but his problems are just beginning. **Key Beats:** - **The Heist:** Rivas helps Arthur steal a ship. They barely escape as Antares security gives chase. - **The Betrayal:** Rivas reveals she’s FMC. *"We can help you. But you’ll owe us."* - **The Return to Mars:** Arthur’s homestead is a **smoldering ruin**. The MCMG has already moved in. His neighbors are gone—some dead, some indentured. - **The Offer:** The FMC’s leader, *Kael*, offers Arthur a deal: help them steal Azra from the MCMG, and they’ll give him his claim back. *"Or you can run. But they’ll find you."* - **The Decision:** Arthur agrees—but only because he has no other choice. *"I’m not fighting for you. I’m fighting for my home."* **Ending Hook:** Kael smiles. *"Smart man. But you should know—Azra doesn’t like to be stolen."* --- ### **Chapter 6: The Vein** **Title:** *"The Things It Remembers"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Arthur learns the full extent of Azra’s mystery and the FMC’s true motives. **Key Beats:** - **The Briefing:** The FMC shows Arthur their intel. The MCMG isn’t just mining Azra—they’re *experimenting* on it. *"They’re trying to make it *stable*."* - **The Warning:** Dr. Zhao (now working with the FMC) warns him. *"Azra isn’t just a metal. It’s a *memory*. It remembers what was here before Mars."* - **The Plan:** The FMC wants to destroy the MCMG’s facility and steal a sample. Arthur’s job is to get them inside. *"You’re the only one who knows how to handle it."* - **The Doubt:** Arthur starts to wonder if the FMC are any better than Antares or the MCMG. *"You’re not fighting for Mars. You’re fighting for control."* - **The Vision:** While prepping for the heist, Arthur touches Azra. For a second, he *sees* something—a city, a civilization, *under* the dust. Then it’s gone. **Ending Hook:** Kael hands him a weapon. *"Tonight, we take back Mars."* --- ### **Chapter 7: The Machine** **Title:** *"The Things It Wants"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** The mission goes wrong, and Arthur discovers Azra’s final secret. **Key Beats:** - **The Infiltration:** Arthur and the FMC team break into the MCMG facility. The place is a nightmare—miners in cages, Azra samples pulsing in tanks. - **The Discovery:** They find the *machine*—a massive, alien-looking device buried beneath the vein. It’s *reacting* to Azra. - **The Betrayal:** The MCMG ambushes them. Rivas is killed. Kael is captured. Arthur barely escapes with a sample. - **The Truth:** The machine isn’t human. It’s *old*. And it’s *awake*. - **The Choice:** Arthur realizes the only way to stop the war is to *destroy* the vein—but that might activate the machine. **Ending Hook:** Voss’s voice crackles over the comms. *"Mr. Neegan. You have something that doesn’t belong to you."* --- ### **Chapter 8: The Gambit** **Title:** *"The Things Left Behind"* **Word Count:** ~1,000 **Purpose:** Arthur’s final choice and the story’s resolution. **Key Beats:** - **The Confrontation:** Voss corners Arthur. *"You don’t understand what you’re holding. Azra isn’t a weapon. It’s a *key*."* - **The Revelation:** Voss wants to *activate* the machine. *"It’s not a threat. It’s an *opportunity*. Mars can be more than a mining colony."* - **The Sacrifice:** Arthur realizes the machine isn’t just a device—it’s a *being*. And it’s *hungry*. - **The Decision:** Arthur triggers the Azra sample, causing a chain reaction. The vein collapses. The machine *screams*. - **The Aftermath:** - Voss is consumed by the energy. - The MCMG and FMC blame each other. Mars is plunged into chaos. - Arthur survives, but his claim is gone. He’s a fugitive. - **The Ending:** - Arthur finds Lena in a refugee camp. She’s free—but Mars isn’t. - The last shot is of the machine’s ruins, still *pulsing*. - A transmission from Earth: *"Unidentified energy surge detected on Mars. Investigate immediately."* **Final Hook:** Arthur looks at his hands. They’re **glowing faintly**. *"Not again."* --- ### **Final Notes** - **Tone:** The story should feel like a cross between *Foundation* (political intrigue) and *Alien* (corporate horror), with a dash of *The Thing*’s paranoia. - **Arthur’s Arc:** From a man who just wants to mine in peace to someone who must decide the fate of Mars. His growth should be in his *agency*—he starts as a pawn, but by the end, he’s making the hard choices. - **Azra’s Role:** It should feel like a *character*—unpredictable, dangerous, and *alive* in some way. The less it’s explained, the better. This plan **serves the original prompt** while avoiding LLM pitfalls—**messy, human, and morally ambiguous**, with a protagonist who **fails, hesitates, and makes hard choices**. Would you like any refinements before drafting?
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
Here are the fully fleshed-out characters, grounded in specificity and human complexity to avoid LLM tropes of flatness or performative depth: --- # **Arthur Neegan** ### **Background & Personality** Arthur is a **first-generation Martian settler**, born in a company crèche to parents who signed 30-year indentures to the Mars Consolidated Mining Guild (MCMG). His father died in a tunnel collapse when Arthur was 12; his mother lasted another five years before "voluntarily" re-upping her contract to pay for medical treatment after a drilling accident. Arthur inherited her stubbornness and her debt. He’s a **mining engineer by necessity, not passion**—good with machines, better with people, but terrible at playing corporate games. He’s spent the last decade working **small, unregistered claims** on the fringes of MCMG territory, just enough to scrape by and send money to his sister Lena, who’s still indentured to the Guild. He’s not a revolutionary; he just wants to **be left alone**. **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - **Hates being touched.** Not in a "trauma survivor" way—just a **lifelong discomfort with physical contact**, especially from strangers. He flinches when Voss claps him on the shoulder. - **Talks to his drill.** Not in a "haunted" way—just **muttering curses and encouragement** while he works, like it’s a stubborn mule. *"Come on, you rusted bitch, just one more hole."* - **Superstitious about numbers.** He won’t start a shift on the 13th, and he **always leaves a single Azra crystal unmined** in a new vein—*"for the vein’s luck."* (He’d never admit this to anyone.) - **Stubborn to a fault.** He’d rather **fix a broken machine with duct tape and hope** than admit he needs help. This has nearly killed him twice. - **Secretly sentimental.** He keeps a **folded-up photo of his parents** in his toolkit, but if you asked, he’d say it’s just to remind him of the drill model they used. **Physicality:** - **Appearance:** Lean but wiry-strong, with **permanent dust ground into the creases of his skin**. His hands are **gnarled from years of wrenching bolts**, with **one missing fingertip** (lost to a misfired charge). His hair is **dark, perpetually greasy**, and he keeps it long enough to tie back but short enough to not get caught in machinery. - **Posture:** **Slouches when he’s tired, stands too straight when he’s lying.** Moves like a man who’s **used to ducking low ceilings**—even in the Antares station, he keeps his shoulders hunched. - **Voice:** **Rough, low, with a dry rasp** from breathing recycled air. He **doesn’t raise his voice**—he just **stops talking** when he’s angry, which is worse. - **Tells:** **Rubs his thumb over his missing fingertip** when he’s thinking. **Cracks his knuckles** when he’s nervous. **Never makes eye contact** when he’s lying. ### **Motives, Allegiances & Relationships** - **Primary Motive:** **Get Lena out of her contract.** He doesn’t care about Mars, the MCMG, or Azra—he just wants his sister free. (He’d sell the vein in a heartbeat if it meant she could leave.) - **Secondary Motive:** **Keep his claim.** Not for the money—for the **principle of it**. He’s spent years **scraping by on the edges of the system**; he’s not giving it up without a fight. - **Allegiances:** **None.** He doesn’t trust the FMC, Antares, or the MCMG. His only loyalty is to **people who’ve earned it**—like Rivas, who’s saved his ass twice. - **Relationships:** - **Lena Neegan (sister):** His **only family**. She’s **smarter than him, more political**, and **resents him** for not getting her out sooner. They **fight like hell** but would **die for each other**. - **Captain Rivas (smuggler):** A **pain in his ass**, but she’s **saved his life twice**. He **doesn’t trust her**, but he **doesn’t have anyone else**. - **Dr. Lien Zhao (physicist):** The only person who **understands Azra** as well as he does. He **respects her**, but he’s **terrified of what she knows**. - **Kael (FMC leader):** A **true believer**. Arthur **doesn’t buy his revolution**, but he **doesn’t have a better option**. ### **Flavour Dialogue / Internal Monologue** **Dialogue (to Voss, when offered the deal):** *"Let me get this straight. You’re offering me a cut of the profits, protection from the MCMG, and a shiny new contract. And all I have to do is sign my name and pretend I don’t see the fine print. That about sum it up?"* *(Pauses, rubs his missing fingertip.)* *"Yeah. No."* **Internal Monologue (when he first touches Azra):** *The crystal was warm. Not like metal—like skin. Like something alive. It pulsed under his fingers, slow and steady, like a heartbeat. For a second, he thought he heard something—voices, maybe, or just the wind in the tunnels. Then it was gone. He jerked his hand back. Stupid. It was just a rock. Just a really, really valuable rock.* --- # **Commander Elias Voss (Antares Envoy)** ### **Background & Personality** Voss is a **company man to his core**—not because he loves Antares, but because he **believes in order**. He grew up in the **Antares Diplomatic Corps’ crèche system**, raised to be **loyal, efficient, and utterly replaceable**. His parents were **mid-level bureaucrats** who died in a **corporate purge** when he was 16; he **never asked why**. He’s **not a true believer in Antares’ mission**—he just **doesn’t see an alternative**. The universe is **chaos**, and the only way to survive is **rules, hierarchy, and control**. He **hates Mars** (too messy, too many variables) but **respects its potential**. Azra isn’t just a resource to him—it’s a **tool to enforce stability**. **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - **Obsessed with symmetry.** His quarters are **perfectly ordered**—pens aligned, bed made with hospital corners. He **hates when things are out of place**. - **Never eats in front of others.** He’ll **sip tea for hours** but never take a bite. (He **doesn’t like the way food feels in his mouth**.) - **Hates physical exertion.** He’ll **take a shuttle to the next room** rather than walk. He **sweats easily**, which embarrasses him. - **Terrible at small talk.** He **doesn’t understand jokes**, but he **laughs anyway**—a **sharp, barking sound** that makes people uncomfortable. - **Secretly fascinated by chaos.** He **keeps a journal** of "interesting" people he’s met—**criminals, rebels, outliers**. He **doesn’t know why**. **Physicality:** - **Appearance:** **Tall, gaunt, with the kind of posture that looks like it’s been starched.** His **skin is pale**, almost translucent—he **avoids sunlight**. His **hair is silver-gray**, cut **military-short**. His **hands are long and delicate**, like a pianist’s. - **Posture:** **Stands too straight, moves too precisely.** He **doesn’t fidget**, but he **taps his fingers in patterns** when he’s thinking. - **Voice:** **Smooth, measured, with a faint Antares accent** (a **clip to his vowels**). He **never raises his voice**, but he **doesn’t need to**—people **lean in to hear him**. - **Tells:** **Adjusts his gloves when he’s lying.** **Blinks too fast when he’s annoyed.** **Smiles with his mouth, not his eyes.** ### **Motives, Allegiances & Relationships** - **Primary Motive:** **Control Azra.** Not for power—**to prevent catastrophe**. He **genuinely believes** that if the MCMG or FMC get their hands on it, they’ll **weaponize it and doom the solar system**. - **Secondary Motive:** **Prove that order works.** He wants to **show Antares that Mars can be tamed**—not for their sake, but because he **needs to believe it’s possible**. - **Allegiances:** **Antares (nominally).** He **doesn’t trust them**, but he **doesn’t have anywhere else to go**. - **Relationships:** - **Arthur Neegan:** **Fascinates him.** He **doesn’t understand** why Arthur **resists so much**—it’s **not logical**. He **wants to break him**, not out of cruelty, but because he **wants to understand**. - **Dr. Lien Zhao:** **Respects her, fears her.** She’s the only person who **understands Azra better than he does**, and that **terrifies him**. - **His Superiors (Antares High Command):** **Doesn’t trust them.** He **follows orders**, but he **keeps secrets**. ### **Flavour Dialogue / Internal Monologue** **Dialogue (to Arthur, when Arthur refuses the deal):** *"You’re making a mistake, Mr. Neegan. Not a moral one—those are subjective. A *logical* one. You have something they want. They will take it. The only question is whether you’ll be compensated for it."* *(Pauses, adjusts his gloves.)* *"Or whether you’ll be disposed of."* **Internal Monologue (when he first sees Azra):** *The crystal was wrong. Not in the way of a broken machine—wrong in the way of a **dream you can’t remember**. It **didn’t belong here**. It **didn’t belong anywhere**. He wanted to **smash it**. He wanted to **worship it*. He settled for **locking it in a box**. --- # **Captain Rivas (Smuggler / FMC Operative)** ### **Background & Personality** Rivas was **born in a smuggler’s hold**, the daughter of a **disgraced MCMG pilot** and a **free trader**. She **grew up on ships**, learning to **pilot before she could read**. She **hates the MCMG** (they **blacklisted her father**) but **doesn’t trust the FMC** (they’re **too idealistic**). She’s **not a revolutionary**—she’s a **survivor**. She’s **charming, reckless, and utterly unreliable**—but she’s **loyal to the people who matter**. She **doesn’t believe in causes**, but she **believes in Arthur** (mostly because he’s **the only person who’s ever told her no**). **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - **Never pays for anything.** She **steals, barters, or talks her way out of bills**. She **doesn’t see this as dishonest**—just **efficient**. - **Hates being still.** She **paces, fidgets, taps her fingers** when she’s sitting. She **can’t sleep in a bed**—she **curls up in a chair or on the floor**. - **Terrible with money.** She’ll **blow her entire cut on a single night of drinking**, then **beg Arthur for a loan**. - **Superstitious about ships.** She **won’t fly on a ship with a woman’s name** (bad luck). She **always knocks on the hull before takeoff**. - **Secretly sentimental.** She **keeps a locket with her parents’ photos**, but she **tells people it’s a good luck charm**. **Physicality:** - **Appearance:** **Short, wiry, with dark skin and a scar across her left eyebrow.** Her **hair is a mess of tight curls**, usually tied back with a **piece of wire**.
